title_en: Advanced Categories: Gharib, Mudraj, Maqlub, and Shaadh
title_ar: الفئات المتقدمة: الغريب والمدرج والمقلوب والشاذ
content_en:
Beyond the basic quality categories of sahih, hasan, and da'if, Nukhbat al-Fikr addresses a range of specialized categories that apply to hadiths with distinctive structural characteristics. These categories represent the accumulated wisdom of hadith scholars in identifying specific types of unreliability or irregularity that require special treatment.

Al-Gharib: The Solitary Report

Gharib (strange or solitary) describes a hadith transmitted by only a single narrator at one or more levels of the chain. Ibn Hajar distinguishes between absolute gharib (solitary at every level of the chain), relative gharib (transmitted by many narrators at some levels but solitary at others), and gharib in content (the wording or meaning is unusual even if the chain is not). The gharib category overlaps with the ahad category but is more specifically about isolation at a particular point in the chain.

Being gharib does not automatically make a hadith weak — a single reliable narrator's report may be perfectly sound. But it does raise the question of why no other narrator transmitted this particular report, which creates a mild presumption that requires investigation. If a hadith is gharib and its single narrator is also weak, the combination is especially problematic.

Al-Mudraj: The Interpolated Report

Mudraj (interpolated or conflated) refers to a hadith in which material from outside the original prophetic statement has been inserted into the narration without clear demarcation. This can happen in two ways: a narrator adds his own explanatory comment to the hadith without clearly marking it as his own, or two separate hadiths are merged into a single narration. Mudraj hadiths are problematic because they confuse the authentic prophetic statement with later human commentary, potentially attributing to the Prophet positions he never took.

Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi wrote a specialized work identifying mudraj hadiths, and Ibn Hajar draws on it. The identification of mudraj requires deep familiarity with the hadith literature — knowing which parts of a narration appear in other chains and which do not, and being able to identify the linguistic styles that distinguish the Prophet's speech from narrators' commentary.

Al-Maqlub: The Inverted Report

Maqlub (inverted) refers to two types of inversion. The first is inversion in the isnad — a narrator's name is moved from one position in the chain to another, or a narrator's father's name is given as his own (or vice versa). The second is inversion in the text — the elements of a hadith are stated in the wrong order, or two parts of a hadith are switched. Maqlub isnads can result from carelessness or from deliberate deception, and distinguishing between the two requires knowledge of the biographical literature.

Al-Shaadh and Al-Munkar: Irregularity and Rejection

The shaadh (irregular) hadith is one in which a reliable narrator contradicts other reliable narrators — a situation requiring the critical scholar to determine which account is correct and which is irregular. The munkar (rejected) hadith is one in which a weak narrator contradicts a reliable narrator — a more serious problem since the strong presumption is that the reliable narrator is correct.

Ibn Hajar's treatment of these categories demonstrates the nuanced hierarchy of evidence that hadith scholars developed: when two narrators conflict, the outcome depends not just on which one is more reliable in general but on the specific type and degree of reliability involved, the nature of the contradiction, and any additional corroborating or opposing evidence available. This nuanced judgment — applying general principles to specific cases — is the core skill of the hadith master (hafiz), and Nukhbat al-Fikr's taxonomy provides its conceptual foundation.
